[https://books.google.com/books?id=h1xcc4cGL5cC&pg=PA37 ''On understanding Japanese religion'', p. 37].</ref> The family chief has the title of ''Uji no kami''<ref name="p67" /> (氏上) although ''uji no osa'' (氏長), ''uji no chōja'' (氏長者), and ''uji no mune'' (氏宗) were also used depending on the time period. Uji no kami|url=https://www.persee.fr/doc/dhjap_0000-0000_1995_dic_20_1_951_t1_0016_0000_2|journal=Dictionnaire Historique du Japon|volume=20|issue=1|pages=16}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Uji (clan)]][[es:Uji (clan)]][[fa:اوجی (خاندان)]][[fr:Clan japonais]][[id:Uji (klan)]][[ja:氏]][[ko:씨 (일본)]][[ru:Удзи (род)]][[sv:Uji (klan)]][[uk:Удзі (рід)]][[zh:日本姓氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Uji (clan)]][[es:Uji (clan)]][[fa:اوجی (خاندان)]][[fr:Clan japonais]][[id:Uji (klan)]][[ja:氏]][[ko:씨 (일본)]][[ru:Удзи (род)]][[sv:Uji (klan)]][[uk:Удзі (рід)]][[zh:日本姓氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Uji (clan)]][[es:Uji (clan)]][[fa:اوجی (خاندان)]][[fr:Clan japonais]][[id:Uji (klan)]][[ja:氏]][[ko:씨 (일본)]][[ru:Удзи (род)]][[sv:Uji (klan)]][[uk:Удзі (рід)]][[zh:日本姓氏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q2575910]] Wikidata name: uji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Aristocratic family groups during the Kofun period of Japanese history (300-538)}} :''This is about the early Japanese kin groups. For other uses, see {{ill|Uji (disambiguation)|en|Uji (disambiguation)|WD=Q422616|lt=Uji (disambiguation)}}.''
{{nihongo|'''''Uji'''''|氏|1=Uji (clan)|lt=Uji||}} were {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}ese kin groups of the [[Kofun period]].<ref>[http://heritageofjapan.wordpress.com/following-the-trail-of-tumuli/rebellion-in-kyushu-and-the-rise-of-royal-estates/uji-clans-titles-and-the-organization-of-production-and-trade/ "Uji clans, titles and the organization of production and trade" at HeritageofJapan.wordpress.com]; retrieved 2013-5-12.</ref>
''Uji'' were similar to the traditional [[Japanese clans|Japanese clan]]s;<ref name="nussbaum1010">{{ill|Louis Frédéric|en|Louis Frédéric|WD=Q726040|lt=Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric}}. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA1010 "Uji"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 1010.</ref> however, the pre-[[Taika Reform|Taika]] ''uji'' did not have many of the characteristics which are commonly understood to be part of Japanese clans.<ref>{{ill|John Whitney Hall|en|John Whitney Hall|WD=Q3297726|lt=Hall, John Whitney}}. (1988). [https://books.google.com/books?id=eiTWWfoyuyAC&dq=uji+clan&pg=PA128 ''The Cambridge history of Japan'', Vol. II, p. 128].</ref> For example, the [[Nakatomi clan]] and the [[Fujiwara clan]] were each ''uji''.<ref name="p67">{{ill|Edmond Papinot|en|Edmond Papinot|WD=Q4141631|lt=Papinot, Jacques Edmond Joseph}}. (1906). ''Dictionnaire d’histoire et de géographie du Japon''; Papinot, (2003). [http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf "Ryūzōji", ''Nobiliare du Japon'', p. 67 [PDF 71 of 80)]]; retrieved 2013-5-12.</ref>
The ''uji'' was not only a social, economic and political unit. It also had religious significance in the 5th–7th centuries.<ref>Kitagawa, Joseph Mitsuo. (1987). [https://books.google.com/books?id=h1xcc4cGL5cC&pg=PA37 ''On understanding Japanese religion'', p. 37].</ref> The family chief has the title of ''Uji no kami''<ref name="p67" /> (氏上) although ''uji no osa'' (氏長), ''uji no chōja'' (氏長者), and ''uji no mune'' (氏宗) were also used depending on the time period. At the beginning of the Heian period ''uji no chōja'' was used most often.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Matsubara|first1=Hideichi|last2=Iyanaga|first2=Shôkichi|last3=Akiyama|first3=Terukazu|last4=Yoshikawa|first4=Itsuji|last5=Fujimura|first5=Michio|last6=Fujimura|first6=Jun'ichirō|last7=Yoshida|first7=Shōichirō|last8=Ishii|first8=Susumu|last9=Iyanaga|first9=Teizō|date=1995|title=36. Uji no kami|url=https://www.persee.fr/doc/dhjap_0000-0000_1995_dic_20_1_951_t1_0016_0000_2|journal=Dictionnaire Historique du Japon|volume=20|issue=1|pages=16}}</ref>
==History== The ''Uji'' formed a decentralized ruling structure.
According to {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=Chinese}} records, the clans divided in rising urban centers occupied the Yamato Plains (the region between the present-day cities of Nara and Osaka). The clans were responsible for the protection and the taxation of these independent territories. Each clan was ruled by a headman or warlord and worshiped its own unique ''[[ujigami]]'' or clan spirit. The most thorough ancient description of the ''uji'' clan system comes from early Chinese records during the [[Kofun period]] (300–552 CE) .<ref>Lu, David. (1997). ''Japan: A Documentary History The Dawn of History to the Late Tokugawa Period'', p. 9.</ref>
As pointed out in the ''{{ill|Book of Wei|en|Book of Wei|WD=Q1269357|lt=History of Wei}}'', the peace was preserved among the "[[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa people]]" as long as a queen, who was a member of the powerful {{ill|Yamato people|en|Yamato people|WD=Q640605|lt=Yamato}} clan, played the role of mediator between the various clans. Since pre-classical Japan lacked a centralized government, an official language and a written body of laws, the [[Shinto]] religious beliefs determined the hereditary lineage of the ''uji'' members.<ref>From the ''Wei zhi'', as reproduced in the original Chinese text, ''Shinwa kara Rekishi e'' (From Myths to History): ''Chuo Koronsha, Nihon no Rekishi'' ( A history of Japan), Vol. 1 (1965), pp. 214–220.</ref>
The powerful Yamato clan, the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=Imperial Family}}, consolidated its power during the late sixth and early seventh century, {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku}}, created the {{ill|Seventeen-article constitution|en|Seventeen-article constitution|WD=Q197248|lt=Seventeen Article Constitution}} in 604 CE. This constitution did not constitute an official legal text but it was an attempt to create a bureaucracy to undercut the political domination of the great clans.<ref>''Nikon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in ''Kokushi Taikei'' (Major compilation of National History), new and enlarged, Vol. 1, No. 2 (1967), pp 142–146.</ref> {{wiktionary|氏}}
==References== {{reflist}}]]

(1906). ''Dictionnaire d’histoire et de géographie du Japon''; Papinot, (2003). [http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf "Ryūzōji", ''Nobiliare du Japon'', p. 67 [PDF 71 of 80)]]; retrieved 2013-5-12.</ref>

[https://books.google.com/books?id=h1xcc4cGL5cC&pg=PA37 ''On understanding Japanese religion'', p. 37].</ref> The family chief has the title of ''Uji no kami''<ref name="p67" /> (氏上) although ''uji no osa'' (氏長), ''uji no chōja'' (氏長者), and ''uji no mune'' (氏宗) were also used depending on the time period. At the beginning of the Heian period ''uji no chōja'' was used most often.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Matsubara|first1=Hideichi|last2=Iyanaga|first2=Shôkichi|last3=Akiyama|first3=Terukazu|last4=Yoshikawa|first4=Itsuji|last5=Fujimura|first5=Michio|last6=Fujimura|first6=Jun'ichirō|last7=Yoshida|first7=Shōichirō|last8=Ishii|first8=Susumu|last9=Iyanaga|first9=Teizō|date=1995|title=36. Uji no kami|url=https://www.persee.fr/doc/dhjap_0000-0000_1995_dic_20_1_951_t1_0016_0000_2|journal=Dictionnaire Historique du Japon|volume=20|issue=1|pages=16}}</ref>

''Japan: A Documentary History The Dawn of History to the Late Tokugawa Period'', p. 9.</ref>

[https://books.google.com/books?id=eiTWWfoyuyAC&dq=uji+clan&pg=PA128 ''The Cambridge history of Japan'', Vol. II, p. 128].</ref> For example, the [[Nakatomi clan]] and the [[Fujiwara clan]] were each ''uji''.<ref name="p67">{{ill|Edmond Papinot|en|Edmond Papinot|WD=Q4141631|lt=Papinot, Jacques Edmond Joseph}}. (1906). ''Dictionnaire d’histoire et de géographie du Japon''; Papinot, (2003). [http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf "Ryūzōji", ''Nobiliare du Japon'', p. 67 [PDF 71 of 80)]]; retrieved 2013-5-12.</ref>
The ''uji'' was not only a social, economic and political unit. It also had religious significance in the 5th–7th centuries.<ref>Kitagawa, Joseph Mitsuo. (1987). [https://books.google.com/books?id=h1xcc4cGL5cC&pg=PA37 ''On understanding Japanese religion'', p. 37].</ref> The family chief has the title of ''Uji no kami''<ref name="p67" /> (氏上) although ''uji no osa'' (氏長), ''uji no chōja'' (氏長者), and ''uji no mune'' (氏宗) were also used depending on the time period. At the beginning of the Heian period ''uji no chōja'' was used most often.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Matsubara|first1=Hideichi|last2=Iyanaga|first2=Shôkichi|last3=Akiyama|first3=Terukazu|last4=Yoshikawa|first4=Itsuji|last5=Fujimura|first5=Michio|last6=Fujimura|first6=Jun'ichirō|last7=Yoshida|first7=Shōichirō|last8=Ishii|first8=Susumu|last9=Iyanaga|first9=Teizō|date=1995|title=36. Uji no kami|url=https://www.persee.fr/doc/dhjap_0000-0000_1995_dic_20_1_951_t1_0016_0000_2|journal=Dictionnaire Historique du Japon|volume=20|issue=1|pages=16}}</ref>
==History== The ''Uji'' formed a decentralized ruling structure.
According to {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=Chinese}} records, the clans divided in rising urban centers occupied the Yamato Plains (the region between the present-day cities of Nara and Osaka). The clans were responsible for the protection and the taxation of these independent territories. Each clan was ruled by a headman or warlord and worshiped its own unique ''[[ujigami]]'' or clan spirit. The most thorough ancient description of the ''uji'' clan system comes from early Chinese records during the [[Kofun period]] (300–552 CE) .<ref>Lu, David. (1997). ''Japan: A Documentary History The Dawn of History to the Late Tokugawa Period'', p. 9.</ref>
As pointed out in the ''{{ill|Book of Wei|en|Book of Wei|WD=Q1269357|lt=History of Wei}}'', the peace was preserved among the "[[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa people]]" as long as a queen, who was a member of the powerful {{ill|Yamato people|en|Yamato people|WD=Q640605|lt=Yamato}} clan, played the role of mediator between the various clans. Since pre-classical Japan lacked a centralized government, an official language and a written body of laws, the [[Shinto]] religious beliefs determined the hereditary lineage of the ''uji'' members.<ref>From the ''Wei zhi'', as reproduced in the original Chinese text, ''Shinwa kara Rekishi e'' (From Myths to History): ''Chuo Koronsha, Nihon no Rekishi'' ( A history of Japan), Vol. 1 (1965), pp. 214–220.</ref>
The powerful Yamato clan, the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=Imperial Family}}, consolidated its power during the late sixth and early seventh century, {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku}}, created the {{ill|Seventeen-article constitution|en|Seventeen-article constitution|WD=Q197248|lt=Seventeen Article Constitution}} in 604 CE. This constitution did not constitute an official legal text but it was an attempt to create a bureaucracy to undercut the political domination of the great clans.<ref>''Nikon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in ''Kokushi Taikei'' (Major compilation of National History), new and enlarged, Vol. 1, No. 2 (1967), pp 142–146.</ref> {{wiktionary|氏}}
==References== {{reflist}}]]

{{nihongo|'''''Uji'''''|氏|1=Uji (clan)|lt=Uji||}} were {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}ese kin groups of the [[Kofun period]].<ref>[http://heritageofjapan.wordpress.com/following-the-trail-of-tumuli/rebellion-in-kyushu-and-the-rise-of-royal-estates/uji-clans-titles-and-the-organization-of-production-and-trade/ "Uji clans, titles and the organization of production and trade" at HeritageofJapan.wordpress.com]; retrieved 2013-5-12.</ref>
''Uji'' were similar to the traditional [[Japanese clans|Japanese clan]]s;<ref name="nussbaum1010">{{ill|Louis Frédéric|en|Louis Frédéric|WD=Q726040|lt=Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric}}. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA1010 "Uji"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 1010.</ref> however, the pre-[[Taika Reform|Taika]] ''uji'' did not have many of the characteristics which are commonly understood to be part of Japanese clans.<ref>{{ill|John Whitney Hall|en|John Whitney Hall|WD=Q3297726|lt=Hall, John Whitney}}. (1988). [https://books.google.com/books?id=eiTWWfoyuyAC&dq=uji+clan&pg=PA128 ''The Cambridge history of Japan'', Vol. II, p. 128].</ref> For example, the [[Nakatomi clan]] and the [[Fujiwara clan]] were each ''uji''.<ref name="p67">{{ill|Edmond Papinot|en|Edmond Papinot|WD=Q4141631|lt=Papinot, Jacques Edmond Joseph}}. (1906). ''Dictionnaire d’histoire et de géographie du Japon''; Papinot, (2003). [http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf "Ryūzōji", ''Nobiliare du Japon'', p. 67 [PDF 71 of 80)]]; retrieved 2013-5-12.</ref>
The ''uji'' was not only a social, economic and political unit. It also had religious significance in the 5th–7th centuries.<ref>Kitagawa, Joseph Mitsuo. (1987). [https://books.google.com/books?id=h1xcc4cGL5cC&pg=PA37 ''On understanding Japanese religion'', p. 37].</ref> The family chief has the title of ''Uji no kami''<ref name="p67" /> (氏上) although ''uji no osa'' (氏長), ''uji no chōja'' (氏長者), and ''uji no mune'' (氏宗) were also used depending on the time period. At the beginning of the Heian period ''uji no chōja'' was used most often.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Matsubara|first1=Hideichi|last2=Iyanaga|first2=Shôkichi|last3=Akiyama|first3=Terukazu|last4=Yoshikawa|first4=Itsuji|last5=Fujimura|first5=Michio|last6=Fujimura|first6=Jun'ichirō|last7=Yoshida|first7=Shōichirō|last8=Ishii|first8=Susumu|last9=Iyanaga|first9=Teizō|date=1995|title=36. Uji no kami|url=https://www.persee.fr/doc/dhjap_0000-0000_1995_dic_20_1_951_t1_0016_0000_2|journal=Dictionnaire Historique du Japon|volume=20|issue=1|pages=16}}</ref>

According to {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=Chinese}} records, the clans divided in rising urban centers occupied the Yamato Plains (the region between the present-day cities of Nara and Osaka). The clans were responsible for the protection and the taxation of these independent territories. Each clan was ruled by a headman or warlord and worshiped its own unique ''[[ujigami]]'' or clan spirit. The most thorough ancient description of the ''uji'' clan system comes from early Chinese records during the [[Kofun period]] (300–552 CE) .<ref>Lu, David. (1997). ''Japan: A Documentary History The Dawn of History to the Late Tokugawa Period'', p. 9.</ref>
As pointed out in the ''{{ill|Book of Wei|en|Book of Wei|WD=Q1269357|lt=History of Wei}}'', the peace was preserved among the "[[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa people]]" as long as a queen, who was a member of the powerful {{ill|Yamato people|en|Yamato people|WD=Q640605|lt=Yamato}} clan, played the role of mediator between the various clans. Since pre-classical Japan lacked a centralized government, an official language and a written body of laws, the [[Shinto]] religious beliefs determined the hereditary lineage of the ''uji'' members.<ref>From the ''Wei zhi'', as reproduced in the original Chinese text, ''Shinwa kara Rekishi e'' (From Myths to History): ''Chuo Koronsha, Nihon no Rekishi'' ( A history of Japan), Vol. 1 (1965), pp. 214–220.</ref>
The powerful Yamato clan, the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=Imperial Family}}, consolidated its power during the late sixth and early seventh century, {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku}}, created the {{ill|Seventeen-article constitution|en|Seventeen-article constitution|WD=Q197248|lt=Seventeen Article Constitution}} in 604 CE. This constitution did not constitute an official legal text but it was an attempt to create a bureaucracy to undercut the political domination of the great clans.<ref>''Nikon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in ''Kokushi Taikei'' (Major compilation of National History), new and enlarged, Vol. 1, No. 2 (1967), pp 142–146.</ref> {{wiktionary|氏}}

As pointed out in the ''{{ill|Book of Wei|en|Book of Wei|WD=Q1269357|lt=History of Wei|1=Book of Wei}}'', the peace was preserved among the "[[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa people]]" as long as a queen, who was a member of the powerful {{ill|Yamato people|en|Yamato people|WD=Q640605|lt=Yamato|1=Yamato people}} clan, played the role of mediator between the various clans. The powerful Yamato clan, the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=Imperial Family|1=Imperial House of Japan}}, consolidated its power during the late sixth and early seventh century, {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}}, created the {{ill|Seventeen-article constitution|en|Seventeen-article constitution|WD=Q197248|lt=Seventeen Article Constitution|1=Seventeen-article constitution}} in 604 CE. This constitution did not constitute an official legal text but it was an attempt to create a bureaucracy to undercut the political domination of the great clans.<ref>''Nikon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in ''Kokushi Taikei'' (Major compilation of National History), new and enlarged, Vol. 1, No. 2 (1967), pp 142–146.</ref> {{wiktionary|氏}}
==References== {{reflist}}]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Uji (clan)]][[es:Uji (clan)]][[fa:اوجی (خاندان)]][[fr:Clan japonais]][[id:Uji (klan)]][[ja:氏]][[ko:씨 (일본)]][[ru:Удзи (род)]][[sv:Uji (klan)]][[uk:Удзі (рід)]][[zh:日本姓氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Uji (clan)]][[es:Uji (clan)]][[fa:اوجی (خاندان)]][[fr:Clan japonais]][[id:Uji (klan)]][[ja:氏]][[ko:씨 (일본)]][[ru:Удзи (род)]][[sv:Uji (klan)]][[uk:Удзі (рід)]][[zh:日本姓氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Uji (clan)]][[es:Uji (clan)]][[fa:اوجی (خاندان)]][[fr:Clan japonais]][[id:Uji (klan)]][[ja:氏]][[ko:씨 (일본)]][[ru:Удзи (род)]][[sv:Uji (klan)]][[uk:Удзі (рід)]][[zh:日本姓氏]]
{{Short description|Aristocratic family groups during the Kofun period of Japanese history (300-538)}} :''This is about the early Japanese kin groups. For other uses, see {{ill|Uji (disambiguation)|en|Uji (disambiguation)|WD=Q422616|lt=Uji (disambiguation)|1=Uji (disambiguation)}}.''
{{nihongo|'''''Uji'''''|氏|1=Uji (clan)|lt=Uji||}} were {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}ese kin groups of the [[Kofun period]].<ref>[http://heritageofjapan.wordpress.com/following-the-trail-of-tumuli/rebellion-in-kyushu-and-the-rise-of-royal-estates/uji-clans-titles-and-the-organization-of-production-and-trade/ "Uji clans, titles and the organization of production and trade" at HeritageofJapan.wordpress.com]; retrieved 2013-5-12.</ref>
''Uji'' were similar to the traditional [[Japanese clans|Japanese clan]]s;<ref name="nussbaum1010">{{ill|Louis Frédéric|en|Louis Frédéric|WD=Q726040|lt=Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric|1=Louis Frédéric}}. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA1010 "Uji"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 1010.</ref> however, the pre-[[Taika Reform|Taika]] ''uji'' did not have many of the characteristics which are commonly understood to be part of Japanese clans.<ref>{{ill|John Whitney Hall|en|John Whitney Hall|WD=Q3297726|lt=Hall, John Whitney|1=John Whitney Hall}}. (1988). [https://books.google.com/books?id=eiTWWfoyuyAC&dq=uji+clan&pg=PA128 ''The Cambridge history of Japan'', Vol. II, p. 128].</ref> For example, the [[Nakatomi clan]] and the [[Fujiwara clan]] were each ''uji''.<ref name="p67">{{ill|Edmond Papinot|en|Edmond Papinot|WD=Q4141631|lt=Papinot, Jacques Edmond Joseph|1=Edmond Papinot}}. (1906). ''Dictionnaire d’histoire et de géographie du Japon''; Papinot, (2003). [http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf "Ryūzōji", ''Nobiliare du Japon'', p. 67 [PDF 71 of 80)]]; retrieved 2013-5-12.</ref>
The ''uji'' was not only a social, economic and political unit. It also had religious significance in the 5th–7th centuries.<ref>Kitagawa, Joseph Mitsuo. (1987). [https://books.google.com/books?id=h1xcc4cGL5cC&pg=PA37 ''On understanding Japanese religion'', p. 37].</ref> The family chief has the title of ''Uji no kami''<ref name="p67" /> (氏上) although ''uji no osa'' (氏長), ''uji no chōja'' (氏長者), and ''uji no mune'' (氏宗) were also used depending on the time period. At the beginning of the Heian period ''uji no chōja'' was used most often.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Matsubara|first1=Hideichi|last2=Iyanaga|first2=Shôkichi|last3=Akiyama|first3=Terukazu|last4=Yoshikawa|first4=Itsuji|last5=Fujimura|first5=Michio|last6=Fujimura|first6=Jun'ichirō|last7=Yoshida|first7=Shōichirō|last8=Ishii|first8=Susumu|last9=Iyanaga|first9=Teizō|date=1995|title=36. Uji no kami|url=https://www.persee.fr/doc/dhjap_0000-0000_1995_dic_20_1_951_t1_0016_0000_2|journal=Dictionnaire Historique du Japon|volume=20|issue=1|pages=16}}</ref>
==History== The ''Uji'' formed a decentralized ruling structure.
According to {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=Chinese|1=China}} records, the clans divided in rising urban centers occupied the Yamato Plains (the region between the present-day cities of Nara and Osaka). The clans were responsible for the protection and the taxation of these independent territories. Each clan was ruled by a headman or warlord and worshiped its own unique ''[[ujigami]]'' or clan spirit. The most thorough ancient description of the ''uji'' clan system comes from early Chinese records during the [[Kofun period]] (300–552 CE) .<ref>Lu, David. (1997). ''Japan: A Documentary History The Dawn of History to the Late Tokugawa Period'', p. 9.</ref>
As pointed out in the ''{{ill|Book of Wei|en|Book of Wei|WD=Q1269357|lt=History of Wei|1=Book of Wei}}'', the peace was preserved among the "[[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa people]]" as long as a queen, who was a member of the powerful {{ill|Yamato people|en|Yamato people|WD=Q640605|lt=Yamato|1=Yamato people}} clan, played the role of mediator between the various clans. Since pre-classical Japan lacked a centralized government, an official language and a written body of laws, the [[Shinto]] religious beliefs determined the hereditary lineage of the ''uji'' members.<ref>From the ''Wei zhi'', as reproduced in the original Chinese text, ''Shinwa kara Rekishi e'' (From Myths to History): ''Chuo Koronsha, Nihon no Rekishi'' ( A history of Japan), Vol. 1 (1965), pp. 214–220.</ref>
The powerful Yamato clan, the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=Imperial Family|1=Imperial House of Japan}}, consolidated its power during the late sixth and early seventh century, {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}}, created the {{ill|Seventeen-article constitution|en|Seventeen-article constitution|WD=Q197248|lt=Seventeen Article Constitution|1=Seventeen-article constitution}} in 604 CE. This constitution did not constitute an official legal text but it was an attempt to create a bureaucracy to undercut the political domination of the great clans.<ref>''Nikon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in ''Kokushi Taikei'' (Major compilation of National History), new and enlarged, Vol. 1, No. 2 (1967), pp 142–146.</ref> {{wiktionary|氏}}
==References== {{reflist}}]]

{{Short description|Aristocratic family groups during the Kofun period of Japanese history (300-538)}} :''This is about the early Japanese kin groups. For other uses, see {{ill|Uji (disambiguation)|en|Uji (disambiguation)|WD=Q422616|lt=Uji (disambiguation)|1=Uji (disambiguation)}}.''
{{nihongo|'''''Uji'''''|氏|1=Uji (clan)|lt=Uji||}} were {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}ese kin groups of the [[Kofun period]].<ref>[http://heritageofjapan.wordpress.com/following-the-trail-of-tumuli/rebellion-in-kyushu-and-the-rise-of-royal-estates/uji-clans-titles-and-the-organization-of-production-and-trade/ "Uji clans, titles and the organization of production and trade" at HeritageofJapan.wordpress.com]; retrieved 2013-5-12.</ref>
''Uji'' were similar to the traditional [[Japanese clans|Japanese clan]]s;<ref name="nussbaum1010">{{ill|Louis Frédéric|en|Louis Frédéric|WD=Q726040|lt=Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric|1=Louis Frédéric}}. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA1010 "Uji"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 1010.</ref> however, the pre-[[Taika Reform|Taika]] ''uji'' did not have many of the characteristics which are commonly understood to be part of Japanese clans.<ref>{{ill|John Whitney Hall|en|John Whitney Hall|WD=Q3297726|lt=Hall, John Whitney|1=John Whitney Hall}}. (1988). [https://books.google.com/books?id=eiTWWfoyuyAC&dq=uji+clan&pg=PA128 ''The Cambridge history of Japan'', Vol. II, p. 128].</ref> For example, the [[Nakatomi clan]] and the [[Fujiwara clan]] were each ''uji''.<ref name="p67">{{ill|Edmond Papinot|en|Edmond Papinot|WD=Q4141631|lt=Papinot, Jacques Edmond Joseph|1=Edmond Papinot}}. (1906). ''Dictionnaire d’histoire et de géographie du Japon''; Papinot, (2003). [http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf "Ryūzōji", ''Nobiliare du Japon'', p. 67 [PDF 71 of 80)]]; retrieved 2013-5-12.</ref>
The ''uji'' was not only a social, economic and political unit. It also had religious significance in the 5th–7th centuries.<ref>Kitagawa, Joseph Mitsuo. (1987). [https://books.google.com/books?id=h1xcc4cGL5cC&pg=PA37 ''On understanding Japanese religion'', p. 37].</ref> The family chief has the title of ''Uji no kami''<ref name="p67" /> (氏上) although ''uji no osa'' (氏長), ''uji no chōja'' (氏長者), and ''uji no mune'' (氏宗) were also used depending on the time period. At the beginning of the Heian period ''uji no chōja'' was used most often.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Matsubara|first1=Hideichi|last2=Iyanaga|first2=Shôkichi|last3=Akiyama|first3=Terukazu|last4=Yoshikawa|first4=Itsuji|last5=Fujimura|first5=Michio|last6=Fujimura|first6=Jun'ichirō|last7=Yoshida|first7=Shōichirō|last8=Ishii|first8=Susumu|last9=Iyanaga|first9=Teizō|date=1995|title=36. Uji no kami|url=https://www.persee.fr/doc/dhjap_0000-0000_1995_dic_20_1_951_t1_0016_0000_2|journal=Dictionnaire Historique du Japon|volume=20|issue=1|pages=16}}</ref>
==History== The ''Uji'' formed a decentralized ruling structure.
According to {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=Chinese|1=China}} records, the clans divided in rising urban centers occupied the Yamato Plains (the region between the present-day cities of Nara and Osaka). The clans were responsible for the protection and the taxation of these independent territories. Each clan was ruled by a headman or warlord and worshiped its own unique ''[[ujigami]]'' or clan spirit. The most thorough ancient description of the ''uji'' clan system comes from early Chinese records during the [[Kofun period]] (300–552 CE) .<ref>Lu, David. (1997). ''Japan: A Documentary History The Dawn of History to the Late Tokugawa Period'', p. 9.</ref>
As pointed out in the ''{{ill|Book of Wei|en|Book of Wei|WD=Q1269357|lt=History of Wei|1=Book of Wei}}'', the peace was preserved among the "[[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa people]]" as long as a queen, who was a member of the powerful {{ill|Yamato people|en|Yamato people|WD=Q640605|lt=Yamato|1=Yamato people}} clan, played the role of mediator between the various clans. Since pre-classical Japan lacked a centralized government, an official language and a written body of laws, the [[Shinto]] religious beliefs determined the hereditary lineage of the ''uji'' members.<ref>From the ''Wei zhi'', as reproduced in the original Chinese text, ''Shinwa kara Rekishi e'' (From Myths to History): ''Chuo Koronsha, Nihon no Rekishi'' ( A history of Japan), Vol. 1 (1965), pp. 214–220.</ref>
The powerful Yamato clan, the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=Imperial Family|1=Imperial House of Japan}}, consolidated its power during the late sixth and early seventh century, {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}}, created the {{ill|Seventeen-article constitution|en|Seventeen-article constitution|WD=Q197248|lt=Seventeen Article Constitution|1=Seventeen-article constitution}} in 604 CE. This constitution did not constitute an official legal text but it was an attempt to create a bureaucracy to undercut the political domination of the great clans.<ref>''Nikon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) in ''Kokushi Taikei'' (Major compilation of National History), new and enlarged, Vol. 1, No. 2 (1967), pp 142–146.</ref> {{wiktionary|氏}}
